speaker: interviewer
text: It is November 17, 1995. The survivor is Freda Weiss, F-R-E-D-A W-E-I-S-S. Name at birth, Marcus, M-A-R-C-U-S. I am the interviewer, Sally Belson, S-A-L-L-Y B-E-L-S-O-N. We are in West Palm Beach, Florida, United States. And the interview will be conducted in English. My name is Sally Belson. I am the interviewer. It is November 17, 1995. The survivor I am interviewing is Freda Weiss. And we are in West Palm Beach, Florida, United States. Could you please tell us your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Freda Weiss.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maiden name is [? Frima. ?] They used to call me [? Frima ?] Marcus. Yiddish, I'm [? Frimad. ?] In the Romanian school I was [? Frima ?] Marcus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday is April 18, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you tell us the city and country that you were born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. I'm born in Ruskova. In Romania they called it Ruskova.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could just spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-U-S-K-O-V-A. This is in Maramures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: M-A-R-A-M-U-R-E-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mrs. Weiss, could you tell us about your childhood, your family? Start by talking about your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was one six children by my parents. My father's name was Mayer Marcus. My mother's name is Rose [? Yankowitz. ?] They were very, very good people. My mother wasn't only a mother for me, but she was just like a friend for me. We weren't very rich at home. But we had a very nice agricultural place, which we raised cows and chicken and gooses, and all kind of those things. We had 200 apple trees in our garden. The house was located in the garden, surrounded by trees, all kind of apples. I could tell you the names, even.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was [? Gold, ?] [INAUDIBLE], Macintosh, Jonathan, and many, many more other names. And in the garden we had-- my father, [? [YIDDISH], ?] he were very, very handy in the agricultural things.

speaker: interviewee
text: He built a house between the trees for bees. He raised bees to make honey. He said there is the apples and there is good children, like I have. There should be sweetness also. There is honey, good. So he raised a bunch of bees, about 100 families. And he built a house for them. Between the trees there was that. Beautiful. People used to come from all over to admire our garden, our honey house, and our bee house. And we had [? meters ?] of honey. And this, from what we made a living. We had another big field but three miles away from our home, which we kept a Russian-- that was Ukraine. Romania, but Ukraine people lived there. And we gave them half of the-- because he was working in our field there. He made hay. We put, there, the cows to eat the grass. And he watched all that. And that's why we paid him half of the income.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so your brothers and sisters all helped, also, work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brothers and sisters helped. One of my brothers became a chalutz. He made--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you-- could you tell us your brothers and your sisters, their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The elder brother, which is now in Israel, is Nsn, in Yiddish, Nathan [? Yankowitz. ?] He made the [INAUDIBLE]. He emigrated with his bride to Israel, in 1936. He went to Israel. So he was there. But until he was home, he was a very good young man. He was working hard. And every Friday when he got a pension from the work, he came to-- he lived separate, not in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he came to my mother. And he put her in her few dollars. Not dollars, there was lei. Romania was lei, L-E-I-- OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lei. That we should have a Shabbos [INAUDIBLE]. And the second is Etu, my sister. She was a very, very good woman, a very nice young lady. And after her came Sarah. Sarah-- and they both got married. And they lived very nicely. They had children, nice husbands, in a religious way. And [INAUDIBLE], which, she calls [? Rosalia, ?] [? Rosie. ?] She lived in Timisoara, Temesvar. She got married to a man by that name Freid, Nathan Freid. And she was very happy. She has three children. One lives in Israel, one in Brooklyn, New York. And the one-- no, the young man is also in Israel. Two children in Israel. And after [? Rosalia, ?] I came. And after me was Menachem Marcus. He's now in Israel. And he wouldn't leave Israel for no money. I would love for him to come to-- to settle here, because his wife died a year ago. And he is very sad. But he wouldn't leave Israel. He's a idealist. And was living with my sisters and brothers, very good. We loved each other very much. We understood each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a religious family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were religious, shomer Shabbos. And it was very nice. I start going to school when I was five years old. I liked, so much, school, that when I finished the eighth grade-- because there wasn't high school or college. Which I am very sorry I don't have the college degree. But it's OK. But this age, what I'm now, I'm satisfied, the way I am. And I went, twice, the eighth grade. Because there was no others. And I liked it so much that I begged the teacher, please, let me go another year to school. And he agreed. He said, OK. I helped a lot with agriculture. We had to go two miles every morning, to pick-- to the cows in the other field. Because there, from where we brought the milk. And I knew to milk-- to milk the cows. And you brought-- walked-- we walked two miles every morning, for to bring the milk home. We had a nice house. In fact, when the Hungarian came in and occupied Romania, in 1939, the notary-- I don't know how you-- the office was in our house. They picked a room of our house. And a notary public-- that's what it was then-- and other secretary lived in our house. We didn't have only two rooms and a kitchen. But one room was for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you, at that time, know about the Nazis? Were you-- was your community feeling anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anti-Semitism, yes. We feel that. Because the Ukrainian, they came into our house. And they said, you know, Mayer-- to my father-- that we going to live in this house, where you live-- where you live now. This is not going to be, anymore, your house soon. Because in-- in Poland, they are killing Jews. They're taking Jews away to Poland, somewhere in a concentration camp. And they are killing them. And little by little, there is a German, a Germany, who calls the Nazis. They will come here in Hungary, also. Because they are already in Czechoslovakia. And they are doing, there, their work. They're picking an the Jews together. They're putting them in a ghetto fist. And from the ghetto they are transporting them to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: That Ukrainian, they knew. But we were laughing from them. We thought, what are they, crazy? What they talking about? Who-- who would do that to human being? Why? Why? We asked the Ukrainian, why? They said, because you are a Jew. That's why. So we opened that door. And we said, out from here. You are a-- you are the anti-- anti-Semite. And you are just making up all those stories. And we you don't believe it. So everybody in your community. Were there a lot of Jewish families in your community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our community had 250 Jewish families.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it was approximately about 2,000 people, Jewish people-- people. Families, it was 250.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any restrictions imposed on you at any point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When the Nazis occupied, when they came-- oh, also when the Hungarian occupied, it was big antisemitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hungarian occupied in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's when you started experiencing--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and yeah. And we experienced, already, the Hungarian-- they-- they didn't like the Jews. They-- they-- they always talked bad words about the Jewish people, how rich the Jews are because they are-- they don't do, the right way, the money. That's what they said. And they were antisemitic. Until 1944. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you sharing your home? How long had they-- did they take over your home for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they-- we left them there. They took us-- they shipped us to the ghetto. And they-- we left the Germans, the Nazis, still, in our home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the day that they came to your home to take you to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date, I know it's 1944. It's-- It was in-- before Pesach. It was around April, April the 18, something like that. Yeah, I remember, it was my birthday. And I said, no! And on my birthday they're going to come and make a-- a mess. And we couldn't celebrate my birthday. That, I remember. April 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: And somebody came to your home that day, to your community? Could you describe what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. All of the sudden, we saw a lot of tanks and cars, big trucks full with people. We didn't know that the Nazis-- we didn't know about that. Because we didn't believe what the Ukrainian said to us. And they run out from that trucks and they-- from the tanks, with those boots, and with those uniforms. And-- and we got very scared. All of a sudden, they went-- they came into our house, knocking with their boots and-- and screaming. And in their hand, they had that stick, iron stick in their hand. They didn't came only to us. The came to every Jewish family's house. But I saw what I saw. And they said, out! Out! Out! Out from here! Out! Out! You have to go!

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, warum, then? In German language, warum, then? Why? He says, you are a Jew! So I said, so what is it, if we are born Jewish? You have to go in the ghettos! And this house has to belong to us!

speaker: interviewee
text: We need it. We don't have where to stay. And out for me! And don't ask a second question. My mother didn't understood what's going on. So I stood near her. And I told her, ma, they want us to go out from the house. They want to occupy the house. Where shall we go, ma ask me. They understood what my mother said. And they showed a little room where we had a storage room. You go there and you stay there until you go in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we didn't say a second word. Because the stick in the hand was going around to hit us. My mother was a sick, sick woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- before-- a few weeks before that happened, she fell on the steps. She brought her ribs, about six ribs. And she couldn't move around. And-- but we did. We went in that little room. We sit down on the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in that room with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, myself, my sister, Sarah. She was married already. She lived in another town. But she was pregnant. And her husband was taken away from her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we ask her to come in our house, not to be alone when-- in time when she's going to give birth. And she listened. She came. So she was. And she had a little baby girl-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --without a doctor. Just a woman, a hebamme, they called her.

speaker: interviewee
text: A woman help. And I was there to help. I was young. I was 20-- even not 24 yet. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who else was in the room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just Sarah, my mama, myself, and the little baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: A little baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, And Chanale. Chanale was three years old. My sister Etu, she lived in a different town, far away from us. She had four children. And when they-- the Nazis took away her husband, she was alone. She had a little grocery store. And she was very, very tired. And she could not tend everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went there to help her out a little bit, before the-- the Nazis came into our house. And then that I there a few weeks. I set to Etu, maybe you give me Chanale, to take her home, to our home.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's going to be easier for you. And we going to have a lot of [INAUDIBLE], a lot of pleasure from that. So she agreed, very much. And I took Chanale. So Chanale was with us, in that little room, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay in that room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three days. And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the three days they said, you have to go. You have to go. We said, where shall we go? You're going to find out. You're going to have [INAUDIBLE], who's going to accompany you. And they going to take you in a place.

speaker: interviewer
text: We-- we said, what place? Where? How can we go with a little baby? The baby was a few months. How can we do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They says, don't talk. I don't allow you to talk much. And if you don't want to be hit by us, then go. And don't ask questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we packed a few things. I put on maybe four, five dresses on myself. I thought, I want to have with me, wherever we go, I should have to change.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister took a lot of diapers and things and-- for Chanale we took things. Packages, we schlepped. We went out on the street. And they picked together all the Jews from all the houses in the street. Everybody was standing outside with their babies, with their whatever. Men, wasn't too many. Because they picked the men before. And they took them in the fields. A lot of us, killed in the fields. A lot had to make the graves themselves. And they shot the men, in the graves. So we were most women and children. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it was almost everybody on the streets. Some were-- they put on the wagons. Because wagons with-- cows schlepped the wagons. Because they couldn't go. And they didn't want to kill them there. So they put them on the wagons. And the younger people, we walked to Felsowiso.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Felsowiso. Hungarian, it's Felsowiso, F-E-L-S-O-W-I-S-O. That's [? Oberwiso, ?] in Yiddish, Felsowiso in Hungarian. There was a city. That was a city. It was about 20 miles away. And we had to walk there. We arrived at Felsowiso in the thousands of people. Not only from Ruskova, but from all surrounding towns, [INAUDIBLE], Petrova, [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE]. A lot of towns was all the Jews together, in that Felsowiso. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were maybe 50, 60 people in one-- in one house. And that house had two rooms, maybe. Some houses had only one room and a kitchen. My sister, Chanale, the baby, my mother, and myself were-- but my son-in-law, my sister's husband's relatives lived there. And they came. And they said, you come with us. Stay with us. So it wasn't too crowded. It was-- eh, it was not bad. And we were in there about six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you wait there--

speaker: interviewee
text: While we were there, yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: What restrictions were imposed on you? Where you allow to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- no. We weren't allowed to go out, even. And not to go to neighbors. Not to-- not to mingle around with nobody. And-- but one day, I went to an SS who was standing there and watching us. And then they made, from our own people, they made couples. They shoot-- look. If we do the right thing, if we don't run away. One day I went  to an SS. And I begged him, let me walk home to take something for the baby. We needed, for the baby, something, medication. And he let me. He said, go ahead. I walked twenty miles to Ruskova. Which I find there, the SS people, who were there. But I find the medication, what I needed. And I walked back to Felsowiso.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you wear--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the last time I was in my hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you wearing anything to show that you were a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, yeah, of course. We had the-- of course. We had, on the arms, we had that Judenrein. Judenrein, they called it the Magen David. You know Magen David?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it said, Judenrein. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That means free of Jews. I came back in the ghetto. My mother was still very sick. And everybody crying. And everybody looking at each other. Chanale, we didn't know then, that my sister, Etu, is no more. Because they took them first, before they took us. So that Chanale didn't see the parents anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After the six weeks, being there with very little food, with very little-- they didn't allow too much food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Nazis walking around everywhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nazis walking around, and watching nobody should run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever brought outside to be counted?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This wasn't, in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, already, in Auschwitz, the counting. After six weeks they picked a few hundred in one time. Because how many went in in the trains. In the trains they could put it in thousands. But the train came, already, full with people from other ghettos. So they took a few hundred from our ghetto. And the Hungarian was the ones who took away everything, what we had with us. The Hungarians was very anti-Semite, just like the Nazis. And my mother, when it cames, we stood in the line. And when my mother came in the line to the Hungarian, they should look at if she has gold, if she has money, if-- whatever she had, rings. Whatever she had, they took away. And they saw her golden teeth in the mouth. She had the real golden teeth. And they weren't false. They want to take out-- not partial, they were steady.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the Hungarians start grabbing her teeth and schlepping and schlepping. And I was screaming and crying. What do you want from my mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those teeth are not false! Those teeth are steady! You cannot take them out! When they hear that, then they let her go. And right away they put us in the-- in the wagons. In the train, like cattles. It was a cattle train. [PAUSES FOR 65 SECONDS]
